summary: Martin Fowler recounts the origins and creation of the Agile Manifesto at Snowbird, Utah in February 2001. The meeting grew out of an earlier XP retreat where participants recognized common ground among various 'lightweight methods.' The seventeen attendees chose 'agile' over 'lightweight' because it better captured the adaptiveness central to their approach. They deliberately created a manifesto as a 'call to arms' with clear values and principles that would distinguish agile from watered-down interpretations of iterative development. Fowler emphasizes that the original authors claimed no special status and intentionally 'launched the ship' for others to steer.
key_points: The manifesto emerged from recognizing common ground among XP and other lightweight methods, not from a planned effort to create a movement
The name 'agile' was chosen because 'lightweight' missed the point - adaptiveness and response to change were the core ideas, not merely being light in weight
The authors deliberately claimed no special status and designed the Agile Alliance to spread leadership beyond the original seventeen
The manifesto was intended as a 'rallying cry' with clear distinctions to prevent the kind of term abuse that had diluted 'incremental' and 'iterative'
Fowler views agile as the best current approach to building software, not as a fixed methodology - if something better emerged, they would adopt it
money_quotes: We decided to call it a manifesto since it was a call to arms and a statement of our beliefs.
Light in weight wasn't the point of these methods, it was just a symptom.
I've seen the terms incremental and iterative abused into all sorts of strange project shapes. I hope the manifesto will make clear what is and isn't agile.
There is nothing particularly special about the seventeen of us who happened to be at Snowbird.
The seventeen manifesto authors had 'launched the ship', but saw no reason to have any special say in agile software's future.
We prefer to do all our work in an agile manner, a desire that's often constrained by our clients.
If we discover a better way to work, we'd adopt it. It's just that, thus far, agile methods seem the best way for us to build useful software.
key_insight: The Agile Manifesto was designed as a clear 'stake in the ground' to prevent the dilution of its core values, yet its authors deliberately avoided claiming ownership of the movement they started.
